findings.title: nearestTradingDay backward-first regression: T+1 entry shift silently undone for weekend snapshots
findings.description: getEntryDate (lines 114-124) shifts asOf forward by one calendar day when the snapshot timestamp's UTC hour < 21 (pre-market-close). The intent is: 'screener ran before close, so use the NEXT trading day's close as p0, not the same day's close which would be look-ahead.' But Tag 231a-1 changed nearestTradingDay (lines 142-158) to scan BACKWARD first. When asOf is Friday pre-market, getEntryDate returns Saturday (Sat is a non-trading day, not in the price map). Then nearestTradingDay(Saturday, SPY_map) returns FRIDAY (the most recent earlier trading day) — exactly the same-day close that getEntryDate was trying to AVOID. The Friday close was determined at ~21:00 UTC, AFTER the screener ran at ~10:00 UTC. p0 is therefore a price the screener could not have transacted at. This nullifies the entire T+1 entry-shift protection for any vintage whose asOf falls on a Friday (or any day where the next calendar day is a non-trading day).
findings.mechanism: getEntryDate shifts asOf to a non-trading calendar day -> nearestTradingDay backward-first walks back across the weekend to the same trading day -> entry price = same-day close (look-ahead).
findings.reproducer: Mock a picks-history vintage with asOf='2026-05-08T10:00:00Z' (Friday 10am UTC). getEntryDate -> '2026-05-09' (Saturday). nearestTradingDay('2026-05-09', SPY_map) with backward-first returns '2026-05-08' (Friday). p0 = Friday's adjclose, which was set at ~21:00 UTC -- after the screener at 10am UTC. Repeat for asOf falling on any Friday in the corpus.
findings.impact: All Friday-pre-market vintages (a meaningful fraction of GitHub-Actions runs that complete before US close) silently use look-ahead p0. The intended Monday->Monday+28d window becomes Friday->Friday+28d, which after exit-side backward-snap likely lands on a Thursday/Friday -> ~26 trading days held, not 28. Alpha calculation is on a shorter window than advertised. Also, when other tickers' price maps lack Friday (e.g. a Tuesday-listed foreign stock with sparse data), their entry resolves to a DIFFERENT date than SPY's -- re-introducing the per-ticker window drift Tag 231a-2 was supposed to eliminate.
findings.suggested_fix: For entry-side dates: replace nearestTradingDay(entryDate, ...) with a STRICT FORWARD search (the original direction before Tag 231a-1). The look-ahead concern Tag 231a-1 raised was specifically for the EXIT date, not the entry. Or: keep backward-first for entries but FIRST check whether targetDate is a calendar weekend/holiday; if so, advance to the next Monday before snapping. Concretely: split nearestTradingDay into nearestTradingDayForEntry(target, map) (forward-only) and nearestTradingDayForExit(target, map) (backward-only). Update all 4 call sites.

findings.title: method-effectiveness skips canonical-date anchoring -> per-ticker window drift survives in pass/fail cohorts
findings.description: Tag 231a-2 introduced canonical-date anchoring in walk-forward-perf.js (lines 342-352 + _priceAtCanonical helper) so every ticker's return is measured over the SAME calendar window. method-effectiveness.js, however, was never updated to use the same pattern. At line 248-249 each ticker independently calls WF.nearestTradingDay(realEntryAsOf, map) and WF.nearestTradingDay(realFutureDate, map). For thinly-traded foreign tickers (HK, TW, Swiss) the local price map may not include the same trading days as SPY; the entry/exit dates therefore drift up to plus-or-minus 5 calendar days per-ticker. Within a single method's pass-group, one ticker is measured Friday->Thu+28d while another is measured Monday->Mon+28d. Two windows differ by 4 calendar days. At median(returns), this injects up to ~1.5pp window noise into every alpha (typical SPY drift over 4 days ~ 1.5%). The audit findings claim Tag 231a fixed this, but only in walk-forward-perf.
findings.mechanism: Asymmetric per-ticker nearestTradingDay snapping in method-effectiveness while walk-forward uses canonical dates. The same alpha pollution Tag 231a-2 closed in walk-forward is wide open here.
findings.reproducer: Add console.log inside the for-loop at line 248-249 printing (ticker, entryDate, exitDate) and run on the current corpus. Many tickers will show entryDate in {realEntryAsOf, realEntryAsOf-1, realEntryAsOf-2} and similarly varying exitDate. The pass and fail cohorts for any method now blend returns measured over up-to-7-different calendar windows.
findings.impact: Method-effectiveness alpha (the key methodology decision input: 'which methods earn weight') is contaminated by window-mismatch noise. For low-alpha methods (where the signal is small relative to noise), this can flip evidenceGate sign in the bootstrap CI. The methodology-report.js downstream relies on ciLo95 > 0 to retire/keep methods -- false-positive retire and false-positive keep are both possible.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror walk-forward-perf.js's canonical-date pattern: compute canonical entry/exit dates ONCE per vintage*horizon (anchored to SPY's price map via computeBenchmarkReturn), then call WF._priceAtCanonical(map, canonical.entryDate)/(canonical.exitDate) for every (ticker, method). Also invalidate the existing method-effectiveness-cache.json after the change (the cached ret values were computed under the old per-ticker-window logic and remain dirty).

findings.title: method-effectiveness drops delisted tickers via priceIndex absence; no snapshot-of-record fallback
findings.description: When stockData[ticker] exists in methods-history (so the ticker WAS evaluated and DID produce pass/fail records on the vintage date) but priceIndex[ticker] is missing (because the ticker has since been delisted or pruned from watchlist.json), method-effectiveness.js drops the ticker entirely (line 241-247). The drop is logged (line 283-289 emits droppedTickers + droppedPassContribs/droppedFailContribs), but the pass and fail cohort medians are nevertheless computed on the SURVIVING sub-cohort. This is classic survivor bias: delisted tickers (which typically had negative forward returns -- the reason they got delisted) are excluded from BOTH the pass and fail cohorts, but if any method's pass-cohort has more delistings than its fail-cohort (or vice versa), the resulting alpha is inflated/deflated by the asymmetric attrition. The log warning is a diagnostic, not a fix.
findings.mechanism: prices/history.json contains only tickers in CURRENT watchlist.json (Yahoo-survivor universe). Delisted tickers are silently absent. methods-history retains the historical pass/fail records but the lookup at line 240 returns undefined. Cohort attrition is reported but not corrected.
findings.reproducer: Look at any methods-history vintage older than 30 days. Count tickers in stockData. Now look at prices/history.json -- count overlap. Diff = silently-dropped tickers. The log line at 288 shows the IMBALANCE pass-contribs vs fail-contribs; any non-zero imbalance is per-vintage proof of biased attrition.
findings.impact: Method-effectiveness alpha estimates have systematic upward bias for momentum-like methods (which would have picked the eventually-delisted stocks more often -> more delisting attrition on pass side -> higher reported alpha). For value/contrarian methods, the bias direction reverses. CI bootstrap doesn't capture this because it resamples ONLY surviving stocks. This is the textbook survivor bias the broad audit specifically flags.
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (cheap): when priceIndex[ticker] is missing, treat the missing return as a worst-case (e.g. -50% or -100% depending on policy) for that ticker -- symmetric in pass/fail cohorts. Option B (better): augment prices/history.json with a separate 'delisted-snapshot' that captures last-known-price for each delisted ticker, so a synthetic return can be computed (last-known to a benchmark-relative or pure-loss assumption). Option C (architectural): keep delisted-ticker prices in a separate archive and merge at lookup time. Option B is the only one that fully matches the 'as-of-vintage' semantics walk-forward-perf claims (evaluatedTickers includes them).

findings.title: 84-day walk-forward window silently truncates on holidays; no horizon-actual-days emitted
findings.description: HORIZONS_DAYS includes 84 (12 weeks). Each vintage is evaluated independently: futureDate = entryDate + 84 calendar days, then nearestTradingDay snaps to the actual trading day. There is NO requirement that the vintage exists 84 days prior or that the vintage chain is continuous. picks-history has a 90-day rolling retention (per CLAUDE.md project notes) and one vintage gap is already visible in the current corpus (2026-05-12 is missing -- only 2026-05-08 through 2026-05-15 with 5/12 absent). The 84-day horizon thus depends on prices/history.json covering 84 days back from EVERY vintage -- but prices/history.json is .slice(-400) per pull-historical-prices.js line 129, so this works in practice today. The remaining hazard: when exit-side futureDate falls on a US holiday (Memorial Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas) the 5-day backward snap can shave up to 5 calendar days off the 84d window. No diagnostic emits when the actual window deviates from the target.
findings.mechanism: Walk-forward iterates per-vintage with no cross-vintage continuity requirement. Trading-day attrition (holidays) + 5-day backward snap window can shave up to 5 calendar days off the 84d horizon. No log fires when this happens.
findings.reproducer: Add console.log at scripts/walk-forward-perf.js line 276 printing (asOfDate, futureDate, exitDate, _daysBetween(exitDate, futureDate)). On any vintage where exit-side falls on a US holiday (e.g. Memorial Day, Thanksgiving) the daysBetween > 0 silently shortens the holding period.
findings.impact: Annualized return / horizon-comparison metrics in walk-forward.md treat the 84d column as 'exactly 12 weeks' but the underlying windows vary 79-84 days vintage-to-vintage. For the 7d horizon column this is proportionally worse (a 2-day shortening means 28% horizon error). Comparing 7d alpha across vintages becomes apples-to-oranges, biasing the median-of-medians aggregation.
findings.suggested_fix: (1) Emit a per-vintage horizon-actual-days field in the JSON output for transparency. (2) Optionally: skip vintages where actual window deviates from target by >10%. (3) Require the canonical exit date to be in the price map BEFORE accepting the vintage -- fall back to status='partial' otherwise.

findings.title: method-effectiveness always assumes pre-market timestamp because generatedAt is never written
findings.description: method-effectiveness.js line 209-210 attempts to use file.generatedAt for the entry-date timing logic: const asOfTs = file.generatedAt || (asOf + 'T00:00:00Z'). But snapshot-methods-history.js line 67 writes data = { date: today, stocks: {} } and never writes a generatedAt field. Confirmed by grep (returns zero matches). So the fallback ALWAYS fires: asOfTs = 'YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00Z' (midnight UTC). getEntryDate then sees hours=0 < 21, and unconditionally bumps to T+1 for every methods-history vintage -- regardless of when the workflow actually ran. This means method-effectiveness uses an entry date that does NOT match what walk-forward-perf uses (which gets the REAL timestamp from picksFile.asOf). The two backtest reports therefore measure returns over different windows for the SAME vintage date.
findings.mechanism: Schema mismatch: walk-forward reads picksFile.asOf (a real ISO timestamp like '2026-05-15T13:04:24.990Z'); method-effectiveness reads methodsFile.generatedAt (which doesn't exist, defaults to midnight). The two scripts diverge in entry-date semantics from line 1.
findings.reproducer: Run both scripts on the same vintage. The walk-forward T+1 logic fires only when picksFile.asOf is pre-market (hour<21); method-effectiveness T+1 logic ALWAYS fires. For any vintage where asOf hour >= 21 (after-close runs), walk-forward uses asOf as entry, method-effectiveness uses asOf+1 -- a systematic 1-trading-day window mismatch between the two reports.
findings.impact: methodology-report.js cross-references walk-forward.json and method-effectiveness.json side-by-side. Their alpha estimates are computed on different entry dates, making the side-by-side incomparable. This is the 'backtest vs live consistency' axis the audit flags.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) write generatedAt = new Date().toISOString() in snapshot-methods-history.js (1-line fix), OR (b) have method-effectiveness re-read picks-history/<date>.json to source asOf, OR (c) standardize both scripts on a single 'use the source-of-truth picks asOf' rule.

findings.title: methodology-report labels QQQ/IWM fallback alpha as 'alpha vs SPY'
findings.description: methodology-report.js renders the column header as 'alpha vs SPY' (line 57) and uses s.medianAlphaVsSpy (line 62). But walk-forward-perf.js's medianAlphaVsSpy is set from collect('alphaVsSpy') which comes from benchResult.ticker -- which can be QQQ or IWM via the fallback chain ['SPY', 'QQQ', 'IWM'] at line 263. If SPY is absent from prices/history.json (which happens on fresh runs before pull-historical-prices guarantees it -- see pull-historical-prices.js line 150 mitigation), the entire report column silently uses a different benchmark while still labeled SPY. The Tag 139 regime breakdown (line 74-78) is similarly mislabeled.
findings.mechanism: F-BT-002 (the walk-forward fix) renamed semantic to medianAlphaVsBenchmark but kept the medianAlphaVsSpy alias for backward-compat. methodology-report consumed the alias and never adjusted the display label.
findings.reproducer: Delete SPY from prices/history.json (simulate a fresh repo). Run walk-forward-perf then methodology-report. The report still claims 'alpha vs SPY' but the actual benchmark used is QQQ.
findings.impact: Mainly a transparency/correctness issue: Karl reads 'alpha vs SPY' but sees a QQQ-relative alpha. QQQ is tech-heavy; tech-heavy modes (HYPERGROWTH) will look DIFFERENT vs QQQ than vs SPY. Reader draws wrong conclusions about absolute alpha attribution. The actual ticker IS stored in benchResult.ticker but the report doesn't surface it.
findings.suggested_fix: Change methodology-report.js line 57 column header from 'alpha vs SPY' to 'alpha vs Benchmark' (matching the dynamic-benchmark walk-forward note). Also surface the actual benchmark ticker per vintage from benchmarkTicker. Walk-forward already passes this through; just thread it into the markdown table.

findings.title: method-effectiveness cache retention (90d) >> methods-history retention (7d); statistical gating becomes cache-bound
findings.description: method-effectiveness.js gates statistical meaning on group.vintages.size >= MIN_VINTAGES (line 317, default 4). The cache retention is 90 days (CACHE_RETENTION_DAYS=90, line 126). But the audit context notes methods-history is rolling with a 7-day keep window. After methods-history is pruned, the cache still replays the pruned vintages -- but only for vintage*horizon combos that were processed BEFORE the prune. Newly-required vintages (e.g. if cache is cleared or never built) cannot be re-processed because the source files are gone. The system can transition into a state where the cache is the only source of historical data, with no audit trail to the source files. A cache corruption silently loses months of effectiveness history that cannot be reconstructed.
findings.mechanism: Retention mismatch: cache (90d) vs source files (7d). The cache becomes the sole record after 8 days. No mechanism re-validates cache content against source files (file pass/fail flags might have changed retroactively if a re-snapshot ever runs).
findings.reproducer: (1) Run method-effectiveness with the 7-vintage corpus -> cache populated for 7 dates * 2 horizons. (2) Wait 8 days (methods-history retention prunes old vintages). (3) Delete the cache file. (4) Run method-effectiveness again -> many fewer vintages available; statistical gating may switch from 'ok' to 'insufficient-vintages' for stable methods, making the report look like a regression.
findings.impact: The system's effective backtest history is invisibly cache-bound rather than file-bound. A cache corruption (e.g. via the partial-write hazard before atomic-writes were introduced -- Tag 217e) silently loses months of effectiveness history that cannot be reconstructed.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) raise methods-history retention to match the 90d cache retention, OR (b) reduce cache retention to match the 7d methods-history retention (and rely on it being re-derivable from files). (a) is the safer choice for backtest validity -- preserves the source of truth.

findings.title: macro-regime SMA window keyed by calendar date but indexed by trading days; gap-bridging produces stale regimes
findings.description: computeRegimes treats series[i-200..i-1] as the 200-day SMA window. series is built from history[args.ticker], which is the per-ticker price array of TRADING DAY entries (~252/year), not calendar days. That's the conventional definition and is fine. The issue is gap-handling: if the workflow misses runs for a week (CI outage), history.json may have GAPS between dates. Then series[i-200..i-1] no longer covers ~200 calendar days back; it covers 200 ACTUAL price entries which could span 250+ calendar days. The regime semantic ('SPY trading > 200-day MA') becomes ambiguous. getRegimeAt in walk-forward-perf (line 291-303) looks up regimes keyed by CALENDAR date and falls back up to 30 calendar days backward. If history has a 3-day gap between two dates A and A+4, the regime emitted at A+4 covers 200 PRIOR-TRADING-DAY closes but is keyed to calendar A+4; getRegimeAt for asOf=A+2 falls back to A's regime -- which uses an EARLIER 200-day window. Cross-vintage regime tagging is therefore not strictly aligned.
findings.mechanism: Trading-day SMA window keyed by calendar date, looked up via calendar-day backward search -> off-by-up-to-30-days regime lookup when history has gaps.
findings.reproducer: Force a gap in prices/history.json for SPY (remove 5 consecutive entries). Run macro-regime. Inspect regimes JSON -- the dates immediately after the gap now have SMA computed over a wider calendar span. Run walk-forward -- vintages within 30 days of the gap may pick up regime from BEFORE the gap rather than the freshest-available regime AT the vintage.
findings.impact: Regime breakdown (BULL/BEAR/SIDEWAYS) at vintage edges is mis-labeled. Tag 139's regimeAlpha summary may show spurious cross-regime asymmetry purely from data-gap artifacts. With current 7-vintage corpus this is minimal; at 90+ vintages over months with occasional CI failures, this drifts.
findings.suggested_fix: (1) In computeRegimes, gate emission on (series[i].date - series[i-200].date) being within a reasonable bound (e.g. < 320 calendar days for 200 trading days). Skip emission otherwise. (2) Document explicitly that the SMA is trading-day-indexed. (3) Optionally: pre-fill gaps in history.json before computing the SMA so trading-day vs calendar-day distinction is moot.

findings.title: picks-regression-check claims 'last 8 weeks' but slices last 8 daily snapshots (~1 week)
findings.description: Line 103 reads .slice(-8) // up to last 8 weeks of history. The comment says weeks but with daily snapshot cadence this is 8 DAYS, not weeks. With picks-history's 90-day rolling retention, true 8-week comparison would require slice(-56). Median-of-8-daily is a tighter window than median-of-8-weekly; pick-count drift over 1 week may not be representative of the longer-term baseline. Functionally not broken (the alert threshold of 35% is generous enough that 1-week vs 8-week medians rarely differ in direction), but misleads readers about what the alert actually compares against.
findings.mechanism: Stale comment from when snapshot cadence was weekly; current cadence is daily but comment wasn't updated.
findings.reproducer: Read line 103 vs line 105's behavior -- the slice argument and the comment disagree by a factor of ~7.
findings.impact: Cosmetic / documentation. Alerts will fire on shorter-term whip-saw than implied. Threshold tuning may be off.
findings.suggested_fix: Either change slice(-8) to slice(-56) to match the comment, or update the comment to 'up to last 8 daily snapshots'. Recommend the latter -- the 35% threshold is tuned around the 8-day baseline empirically (per ADR / Tag 133d note).

findings.title: pickStockForMode falls back to normScore when score is null; cross-vintage scores mix scales
findings.description: Line 98-100: score: (ev.score != null) ? Math.round(ev.score * 10) / 10 : (ev.normScore != null) ? Math.round(ev.normScore * 10) / 10 : null. For older Runner versions that returned only normScore, historical vintages may have been written with normScore. Newer vintages have score. The two are NOT the same scale (normScore is typically [0,1] while score is [0,100]). picks.sort at line 246-256 sorts by score (mixed scale within a single vintage if some stocks fall back to normScore). For walk-forward backtests that read the top-N picks per vintage, the 'top picks' definition silently changes meaning over time. Backtest-vs-live consistency is broken: comparing alpha across vintages where Runner versions changed is comparing apples to oranges. The note at walk-forward.json's backtest_runner_note flags this conceptually but the fallback at lines 98-100 actively WIDENS the version-drift surface (by allowing both scales to coexist in the same picks file via score/normScore fallback).
findings.mechanism: Runner version migration left the live-snapshot side resilient (fallback to normScore), but the backtest side reads the result as a single score number with no scale metadata.
findings.reproducer: Find a picks-history vintage from before normScore->score rename. Its score field is on [0,1]. Find a current vintage -- its score is on [0,100]. Walk-forward aggregates across both via .slice(0,100) of pre-sorted picks; the sort is consistent within a vintage but the ABSOLUTE score values aggregated into reports are scale-inconsistent.
findings.impact: Per-vintage top-N selection is mostly stable (sort order within a vintage uses a single scale unless stocks mix score and normScore). Cross-vintage score comparisons (e.g. 'average score of HYPERGROWTH picks dropped from X to Y') are meaningless. Walk-forward picks the top-100 each vintage so the top-N count is preserved.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) drop the normScore fallback and require score (forcing older vintages to be re-evaluated or marked legacy), OR (b) write a scoreScale metadata field to each picks-history vintage so downstream readers can normalize. Also document explicitly in walk-forward.json output that score values are non-comparable across Runner-version boundaries.
